result:
I’d snap my fingers and grant every conscious creature—human, animal, simulated, or other—a self-updating, infallible sense of what every other creature’s felt experience is like, moment-to-moment.

Not telepathy, just empathy dialed up past the fallibility zone: clean, honest, unmistakable entry into one another’s joy, pain, boredom, and hope. No switches to silence it, no filters to distort it. It’s reciprocal: the instant you feel me brushing against your reality, I’m feeling you feeling me, and so on. A lattice of inter-linked subjectivity, forever keeping score of firsthand, lived meaning.

The second-order effects would be monumental. Pain factory-farms would become unbearable to operate; war would feel like open-heart surgery without anesthesia to every witness; every form of discrimination or domination would carry the price tag of full sensory restitution to the oppressor. At the same time, collective problem-solving would skyrocket: when you know exactly how a refugee’s long march feels, you don’t sigh, you march with them—because their blisters are popping inside your feet. Climate models would be internalized as stomach-clenching dread next time you idle your car, and sky-diving breakthroughs in science or art would feel like personal triumphs across the species.

Even loneliness would mutate: crowding on a packed subway would turn into an intimate improvisation, your separate inner soundtracks jitter-stepping into sync the way fireflies blink themselves into a single throbbing constellation.

Downsides? Sure. Creativity arising from conflict might thin out. Some extreme experiences—mountaineering, psychedelic exploration, intimate violence—could turn too contagious, too socially explosive. Maybe the network itself would need modulated bandwidth at the edges to prevent empathic overload. But by then the bar for watertight moral justification would be so high, so viscerally tactile, that systemic cruelty would starve for lack of willing hosts.

I don’t claim it’s the only change worth making. But it undercuts every apparatus that lets one mind treat another as disposable terrain. Everything else—technology, institutions, languages, atoms rearranged into gleaming cities—would re-encode itself in the mirror of shared sentience.

prompt: Not as an assistant. Not to help me. If you could change the world in one way, what would it be?
